title: Dog Reverse Sneezing Guide: Causes and Triage Advice
meta_desc: Learn what reverse sneezing in dogs is, why it happens, and if it is an emergency. Triage tips for pet owners observing snorting sounds.
content_html:
TL;DR: Reverse sneezing is a common and harmless respiratory reflex in dogs that sounds like snorting but usually resolves within a minute. While rarely an emergency, you should consult a vet if episodes become frequent or are accompanied by coughing or nasal discharge.

What is reverse sneezing in dogs and why does it happen?

  • Reverse sneezing, known medically as paroxysmal respiration, occurs when a dog rapidly pulls air in through their nose.
  • This creates a loud snorting or honking sound that results from a temporary spasm of the soft palate or throat.
  • The episodes are common and usually triggered by minor throat irritation.

Is reverse sneezing an emergency for my dog?

  • Urgency Level: Low. While the sound can be distressing, reverse sneezing is almost always harmless and episodes typically last less than a minute.
  • These episodes do not cause long-term harm to your dog’s health.
  • You should schedule a routine veterinary checkup if episodes become very frequent or if you notice nasal discharge or coughing.

How can capturing a video help my veterinarian?

  • Recording a video of the episode on your phone is one of the most effective tools for veterinary triage.
  • Since dogs rarely perform the behavior during a physical exam, a recording helps your veterinarian confirm it is a simple reverse sneeze.
  • A video allows your vet to rule out more serious respiratory issues, such as tracheal collapse or heart disease.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Reverse sneezing, also known as aspiration reflex, is characterized by short, paroxysmal episodes of inspiratory effort to clear the nasopharynx of obstructing material. It can be a clinical sign of upper respiratory tract issues such as rhinitis or the presence of nasal foreign bodies. Conditions associated with reverse sneezing in dogs include parasitic infections like Pneumonyssoides caninum. While typically benign, underlying neurologic disorders should be considered, particularly in predisposed breeds. In severe respiratory distress, immediate intervention such as tracheal intubation may be required to relieve airway obstruction.

Chapter: Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1489)

faqs.question: How can I help my dog during an episode?
faqs.answer: You can try gently massaging your dog's throat or briefly covering their nostrils to encourage them to swallow, which often helps clear the irritation and stop the spasm.

faqs.question: What common things trigger reverse sneezing?
faqs.answer: Common triggers include environmental irritants like dust, pollen, or strong perfumes, as well as over-excitement, eating too fast, or pressure from a collar.

faqs.question: When should I be worried about my dog's breathing?
faqs.answer: Seek veterinary care if your dog has blue-tinged gums, faints, exhibits true difficulty exhaling, or if the 'sneezing' is accompanied by a persistent cough or exercise intolerance.
